findings.title: Vault NFTs can be bought for the same flat fee regardless of collection or value

best_agent_match.title: Any NFT held by the vault can be bought for the same tiny flat fee

findings.title: `buyJay()` accepts zero NFTs but still gives the higher 97% mint rate

best_agent_match.title: The NFT-seller mint path works with zero NFTs, bypassing the higher no-NFT buy fee

findings.title: Reentrant `sell()` calls over-withdraw ETH by pricing later sells before prior dev fees leave the pool

best_agent_match.title: Reentrant sells can over-withdraw ETH because seller payout happens before the dev fee transfer

findings.title: Burning the final JAY supply reverts because the post-burn price event divides by zero

best_agent_match.title: Burning the entire remaining supply reverts because the post-burn price event divides by zero

rejected_candidates.title: Anyone can manipulate the stored JAY-denominated NFT redemption fee by skewing spot reserves before `updateFees()`
rejected_candidates.reason: Plausible but too speculative as a standalone issue here: the attacker must first distort pool reserves with their own capital, and the incremental harm is largely subsumed by the already-critical flat-fee NFT theft vector.

rejected_candidates.title: Division by zero in ETHtoJAY pricing function
rejected_candidates.reason: `ETHtoJAY()` is called in payable contexts where `address(this).balance` already includes `msg.value`, so the denominator is effectively the pre-call balance. With the constructor seeding ETH and no realistic path to a zero pre-balance, the claimed division-by-zero case is not practically reachable.

rejected_candidates.title: No access control on updateFees function
rejected_candidates.reason: Permissionless fee updates appear intentional, and the function still constrains non-owner updates with the fee-swing check. The generic lack-of-access-control claim is not reportable by itself.

rejected_candidates.title: No oracle staleness check in price feeds
rejected_candidates.reason: This could at most delay or skew fee refreshes, but the submission does not establish realistic protocol-level fund loss or lockup from stale ETH/USD data in this design.

rejected_candidates.title: Reentrancy vulnerability in buyJay function
rejected_candidates.reason: The only external ETH send is to the configurable `dev` address, which is owner-controlled and already privileged. The candidate does not show an untrusted reentrancy path creating additional protocol harm.

rejected_candidates.title: Unchecked ERC721 transfer return value
rejected_candidates.reason: Standard ERC721 `transferFrom` and `safeTransferFrom` do not return booleans; they revert on failure. There is no ignored return value vulnerability here.

rejected_candidates.title: Unlimited token minting allows inflation
rejected_candidates.reason: Minting against ETH deposits is core protocol behavior, not an unintended unlimited-mint bug.

rejected_candidates.title: No array length validation in buyNFTs
rejected_candidates.reason: Mismatched arrays either revert atomically or let the caller overpay themselves. This is user-input validation quality, not a realistic protocol-level exploit.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing return value check on ERC1155 transfers
rejected_candidates.reason: ERC1155 `safeTransferFrom` also does not return a boolean. Failures revert, so there is no ignored-return-value issue.

rejected_candidates.title: No slippage protection on NFT purchases
rejected_candidates.reason: `buyNFTs()` is not a variable-price swap path; the caller explicitly chooses the NFTs and pays fixed protocol fees. The claimed slippage issue does not fit the code's behavior.

rejected_candidates.title: Owner can change dev wallet before locking
rejected_candidates.reason: This is an explicit owner privilege provided by design until `lockDevWallet()` is used, not an unintended vulnerability.

rejected_candidates.title: Potential integer overflow in SafeMath operations
rejected_candidates.reason: This is not a vulnerability; SafeMath is redundant in Solidity 0.8+ but remains safe.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing event emissions for critical state changes
rejected_candidates.reason: Lack of events is an observability issue, not a realistic protocol-level security finding under the stated reporting bar.
